coredata.dc:title: Cetuximab-conjugated iodine doped carbon dots as a dual fluorescent/CT probe for targeted imaging of lung cancer cells
coredata.dc:creator: Su, Huifang
Liao, Ying
Wu, Fengshou
Sun, Xinzhi
Liu, Hongjian
Wang, Kai
Zhu, Xunjin
coredata.dc:description: Iodine doped carbon quantum dots (I-CQDs) have been synthesized by a facile one-pot hydrothermal method using citric acid and iohexol as precursors. The morphology and chemical structures of I-CQDs are investigated by TEM, XRD, XPS, and FTIR spectroscopy. The as-prepared I-CQDs exhibit excitation-dependent PL behavior with the emission quantum yield of 18%. The presence of iodine ions in I-CQDs is confirmed by XPS spectrum, which endows the composite with CT imaging performance. Thus, they could be used as efficient probes for fluorescence/CT bimodal imaging. To realize a precise diagnosis of tumor lesions, the surface of I-CQDs is conjugated with a targeting molecular (cetuximab) to afford I-CQDs-C225. The MTT assay against three kinds of human cell lines verifies the low cytotoxicity of I-CQDs-C225. The targeting ability of I-CQDs-C225 are evaluated in vitro using HCC827 cells (lung cancer cell line, over-expression of EGFR), H23 (lung cancer cell line, low expression of EGFR) and HLF cells (lung normal cell line, low expression of EGFR) via a confocal laser scanning microscope. The results show that HCC827 cells exhibited strong fluorescence, indicating the cetuximab-conjugated I-CQDs could target specifically the cancer cells with over-expression of EGFR via EGFR mediated endocytosis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Bioimaging
CT
Targeting

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The calculation of quantum yields of I-CQDs
Cytotoxicity assay of I-CQDs and I-CQDs-C225
In vitro imaging and subcellular localization of I-CQDs-C225
Structure of the I-CQDs and I-CQDs-C225

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural Science Foundation of Hubei Province
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Hubei Province

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We are thankful for the support from National Natural Science Foundation of China (21601142) and Natural Science Foundation of Hubei Province (2017CFB689). X. Zhu thanks Hong Kong Research Grants Council (HKBU 22304115-ECS) and the financial support from Hong Kong Baptist University (RC-IRMS/16/17/02CHEM, RC-ICRS/15-16/02E and FRG2/14-15/034).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Proposed formation pathway of I-CQDs and the schematic illustration of the preparation of cetuximab-conjugated I-CQDs (I-CQDs-C225).

TEM image of I-CQDs (A) (inset in picture shows the crystal lattice of I-CQDs) with corresponding size distribution histograms (B); Atom Force Microscopy (AFM) image of I-CQDs (C); EDX chemical mapping of I-CQDs on a TEM copper grid (D); TEM image of I-CQDs-C225 (E) (inset in picture shows the crystal lattice of I-CQDs-C225) with corresponding size distribution histograms (F); Particle size distribution of I-CQDs-C225 measured by dynamic light scattering (G).
XRD pattern of I-CQDs (A); The FT-IR spectrum of I-CQDs (B); XPS survey spectrum (C), and C 1s (D), N 1s (E), and I 3d (F) high-resolution XPS spectra of I-CQDs.

(A) UV−vis absorption spectrum (black line), fluorescence excitation (blue line, λem = 450 nm), and emission spectra (red line, λex = 380 nm) of I-CQDs dispersed in water at room temperature. (The inserts show the photograph of CQDs under 365 nm excitation (right) in comparison to the blank control (left)); (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of I-CQDs obtained at different excitation wavelengths with a 20 nm increment from 280 to 500 nm; (C) UV−vis absorption spectrum (black line) and emission spectra (red line, λex = 380 nm) of the C225-I-CQDs dispersed in water at room temperature. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

(A) Cell viability values (%) estimated by MTT assay versus incubation concentrations of I-CQDs (0, 10, 50, 100, 200, 300, 400 and 500 μg/mL) at 37 °C for 24 h; (B) The confocal fluorescence microscopy images of HeLa cells treated with 0.2 mg/mL of I-CQDs (a–d) for 24 h. Images were taken under (a) a bright field, (b) 405 nm excitation, (c) 458 nm excitation, and (d) 514 nm excitation; (C) Laser scanning confocal microscopy images of HeLa cells incubated with I-CQDs for 24 h. I-CQDs was excited with 514 nm laser. Lysosomes (green) were stained with LysoTracker® Green DND-26 (50 μM). The nucleus (blue) was stained with Hoechst 33342 (1 μg/mL). (The scale bar is 20 μm). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).
The measured CT values of I-CQDs as a function of iodine concentration.

(A) Cell viability of HCC827, H23 and HLF cells after incubation with different concentrations of I-CQDs-C225 (0, 1, 2, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100, 200 and 400 μg/mL) for 24 h; (B) Laser scanning confocal microscopy images of HCC827 cells (overexpress EGFR), H23 cells (low EGFR), and HLF cells (normal lung cells), incubated with I-CQDs-C225 for 6 h. I-CQDs-C225 was excited with 405-nm laser (The scale bar is 20 μm); (C) Laser scanning confocal microscopy images of HCC827 cells incubated with I-CQDs-C225 for 6 h and then stained with LysoTracker® Green DND-26 (50 μM) for 15 min. I-CQDs-C225 was excited with 405-nm laser and LysoTracker® Green was excited with 488 nm laser. (The scale bar is 20 μm). (For interpretation of the references to color in the text, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

head.ce:title: Cetuximab-conjugated iodine doped carbon dots as a dual fluorescent/CT probe for targeted imaging of lung cancer cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Orthopaedics, The First Affiliated Hospital of Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou, 450052, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The First Affiliated Hospital of Zhengzhou University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Green Chemical Process of the Ministry of Education, School of Chemical Engineering and Pharmacy, Wuhan Institute of Technology, Wuhan, 430205, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Green Chemical Process of the Ministry of Education
School of Chemical Engineering and Pharmacy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Environmental and Biological Analysis and Department of Chemistry, Hong Kong Baptist University, Waterloo Road, Hong Kong, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Environmental and Biological Analysis and Department of Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Public Security Technology, Railway Police College, Zhengzhou, 450053, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Key Laboratory for Green Chemical Process of the Ministry of Education, School of Chemical Engineering and Pharmacy, Wuhan Institute of Technology, Wuhan, 430205, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Green Chemical Process of the Ministry of Education
School of Chemical Engineering and Pharmacy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Iodine doped carbon quantum dots (I-CQDs) have been synthesized by a facile one-pot hydrothermal method using citric acid and iohexol as precursors. The morphology and chemical structures of I-CQDs are investigated by TEM, XRD, XPS, and FTIR spectroscopy. The as-prepared I-CQDs exhibit excitation-dependent PL behavior with the emission quantum yield of 18%. The presence of iodine ions in I-CQDs is confirmed by XPS spectrum, which endows the composite with CT imaging performance. Thus, they could be used as efficient probes for fluorescence/CT bimodal imaging. To realize a precise diagnosis of tumor lesions, the surface of I-CQDs is conjugated with a targeting molecular (cetuximab) to afford I-CQDs-C225. The MTT assay against three kinds of human cell lines verifies the low cytotoxicity of I-CQDs-C225. The targeting ability of I-CQDs-C225 are evaluated in vitro using HCC827 cells (lung cancer cell line, over-expression of EGFR), H23 (lung cancer cell line, low expression of EGFR) and HLF cells (lung normal cell line, low expression of EGFR) via a confocal laser scanning microscope. The results show that HCC827 cells exhibited strong fluorescence, indicating the cetuximab-conjugated I-CQDs could target specifically the cancer cells with over-expression of EGFR via EGFR mediated endocytosis.

ce:section.ce:para: Molecular imaging has been considered as one of the most advanced technologies for disease diagnosis in recent years. Using relatively inexpensive and portable equipment, optical imaging with fluorophores permits real-time detection of cancer. Among fluorescent materials, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted more and more interest because of their unique stability and biocompatibility [1–3]. Unlike organic fluorescent dyes, the CQDs have excellent chemical stability and photostability [4–6]. Without heavy metals, they are far less toxic than semiconductor quantum dots [7,8]. However, fluorescence imaging suffers from the general limitations of whole-body optical imaging techniques such as poor spatial resolution and shallow tissue penetration [9]. To overcome this inherent limitation, it is necessary to develop multi-modal imaging probes through incorporation of other imaging modalities such as magnetic resonance imaging, photoacoustic imaging or computed tomography imaging [10,11]. As one of the most reliable molecular imaging modes, computed tomography (CT) has been widely used because of its high spatial and density resolution [12]. For CT imaging, contrast agents are generally required to increase the density of the area to be imaged to improve the diagnostic accuracy [13]. The commercially available CT imaging agents used in clinics are usually iodinated small molecules that have severe drawbacks such as renal toxicity at a high concentration, short imaging time, and nonspecificity [14]. To overcome these drawbacks, it is essential to encapsulate the iodinated compounds within a nanoparticle system or conjugate the compounds with polymers to lower the toxicity and prolong the blood circulation time of the contrast agents [15]. Carbon dots as a new type of fluorescent nanoparticles could be used as the high-efficiency vehicles for CT imaging agents due to their abundant surface groups [16–19]. Hence, it would be advantageous to incorporate the iodinated compounds into carbon dots to realize fluorescence/CT bimodal imaging.

On the other hand, to be a potential molecular imaging probe for diagnosis, it is necessary for the nanoparticles to reach the final destination [20]. The presence of different receptors on the membrane of cancer cells has offered an opportunity to search for the potential route for the delivery of imaging agents [21]. A large number of receptor binding molecules are widely reported, such as folic acid (FA) [22], hyaluronic acid (HA) [23], peptides [24], antibodies, and aptamers, which have been investigated well. Among them, the monoclonal antibodies were extensively used as targeting molecular because of its biocompatible, biodegradable, and nontoxic behavior. For example, C225 (also known as cetuximab) can specially bind to EGF receptor, which is overexpressed among many cancers, such as lung cancer and head and neck cancers and inhibit the growth of the cells [25].

Herein, we report a facile “one-pot” hydrothermal method to prepare iodine-doped CQDs for fluorescence/CT bimodal imaging using citric acid and iohexol as precursors. The as-prepared I-CQDs were characterized and analyzed in detail using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Atomic Force microscopy(AFM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) and fluorescence spectroscopy. The cytotoxicity of I-CQDs against HeLa cells was investigated through standard MTT assay. Due to the presence of iodine element, the luminescent I-CQDs could serve as efficient probes for cellular multicolor imaging and CT imaging. Moreover, to realize the precise diagnosis of tumor lesions, a well-known targeting moiety (cetuximab) was introduced on the surface of I-CQDs to afford cetuximab-conjugated iodine-doped carbon dots (I-CQDs-C225). The targeting ability and subcellular localization of I-CQDs-C225 were then evaluated in vitro using HCC827 cells (over-expression of EGFR), and H23 and HLF cells (low expression of EGFR) through a confocal laser scanning microscope.

Citric acid (CA), and PEG 1500N were purchased from Dieckmann Corporation. Iohexol was purchased from GE Healthcare Company. All chemicals were used as received without any further purification. Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a Bruker Vertex 70 spectrometer. AFM tapping-mode images were acquired using an AFM (NanoScope IIIA Digital Instrument). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using an SKL-12 spectrometer modified equipped with a VG CLAM 4 multichannel hemispherical analyzer. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were recorded on a Perkin-Elmer LS 55 spectrofluorometer. The electronic absorption spectra in the UV/Vis region were recorded using a Hewlett Packard 8453 UV/Vis spectrophotometer. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analyses were performed on an FEI-TECNAI G2 S-TWIN TEM, operating at an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. X-ray powder diffraction measurements were investigated using a Bruker D8 Advance diffractometer through nonmonochromatic Cu Ka X-rays at 40 kV and 40 mA. CLSM imaging were performed on Carl Zeiss LSM 710.

First, 500 mg iohexol and 3.0 g citric acid were diluted with 20 mL water under vigorous stirring to form a transparent homogeneous solution. This solution was heated to 80 °C for 2 h, then heated to 200 °C by oil bath heating. The mixture was heated to evaporate most water to obtain a uniform pale-yellow gel, followed by the addition of 1 mL cold water under heating. The same procedure was repeated for a period of 3 h, until the color of gel turned to yellow. After cooling to room temperature, the reaction mixture was diluted in water and then centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 30 min to remove the large particles. The brown-yellow supernatant was transferred into a dialysis membrane (MWCO 3500) and was dialyzed against ultrapure water for 3 days to remove residues. The dialysis solution was collected and freeze-dried to yield light yellow powder.

The I-doped CQDs powders obtained were passivated with PEG 1500N at 120 °C with the presence of nitrogen gas. The excess amount of PEG 1500N was removed by membrane dialysis (MWCO 3500). PEG-coated I-CQDs (I-CQDs-NH2) terminated with amine groups were conjugated with cetuximab using a modification of the standard EDC–NHS reaction. Typically, 50 μL of cetuximab solution was dissolved in 2 mL of phosphate buffered saline (PBS) (pH = 7.4) solution, followed by the addition of 3 mg EDC and 4 mg sulf-NHS for activation at 4 °C for 30 min. 30 mg I-CQDs-NH2 in 5 mL PBS (pH = 7.4) were added and the mixed solution was allowed to react at 4 °C for 12 h. The resulting suspension was centrifuged for 10 min to remove weight precipitate, and then was dialyzed against DI water for three days at 4 °C. The I-CQDs-C225 was lyophilized and re-dissolved in phosphate buffer (pH 7.4) for further characterization and application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The calculation of quantum yields of I-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The quantum yield of I-CQDs was measured with quinine sulfate as the reference (0.1 M H2SO4 aqueous solution, fluorescent quantum yield ∼ 54%) by the following equation:
φ = φst(I/Ist)(η/ηst)2
Where Φ is fluorescence quantum yield, I is the slope of curves, and η is the refractive index of solvent. The subscript “st” refers to the reference of known quantum yield (quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4). The absorption was kept below 0.1 at the excitation wavelength of 360 nm to minimize reabsorption.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity assay of I-CQDs and I-CQDs-C225
ce:section.ce:para: Cells (6 × 103 cells per well) were seeded in 96-well plates and incubated at 37 °C in a humidified 5% CO2 atmosphere. After 24 h, the cells were exposed to I-CQDs (0–0.5 mg/mL) or I-CQDs-C225 with different concentrations (0–0.4 mg/mL) and further incubated for 24 h. The cells were rinsed with PBS (pH 7.4) and treated with 10 μL MTT (5 mg/mL, in fresh medium), and then incubated for another 4 h. The formazan crystals formed was dissolved in DMSO. The optical absorbance at 570 nm of each solution was measured to calculate the cell viability values.

Cellular imaging was evaluated using a confocal laser scanning microscope. HeLa cells (5 × 104 cells per well) were seeded in 6-well culture plates and allowed to adhere for 48 h. The cells were then treated with I-CQDs (0.2 mg/mL) or I-CQDs-C225 (0.1 mg/mL) at 37 °C for 24 h. After that, the supernatant was carefully removed and the cells were washed three times with PBS. Subsequently, the slides were mounted and observed by confocal microscope (Zeiss Laser Scanning Confocal Microscope; LSM7 DUO) using ZEN 2009 software (Carl Zeiss).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro imaging and subcellular localization of I-CQDs-C225
ce:section.ce:para: The specific binding ability of I-CQDs-C225 for HCC827, H23 or HLF cells was tested via a confocal microscope. The cells above were seeded in a 35 mm Petri dish with a glass cover slide in 2 mL corresponding cell culture medium. After two-day culture, cells were stained with I-CQDs-C225 (100 μg/mL) for predetermined time intervals. Before imaging on the confocal microscope, the cells were washed with PBS three times.

For co-staining of I-CQDs-C225 and Lyso-tracker Green: HCC827 cells were first incubated with I-CQDs-C225 (100 μg/mL) for 6 h at 37 °C, then further incubated with Lyso-tracker Green (50 nM) for 15 min at 37 °C. Before imaging on the confocal microscope, the cells were washed with PBS three times.

To study the X-ray attenuation in vitro, I-CQDs with different concentration ranging from 0 to 0.65 mg/mL were diluted into 100 μL water and added to a 96-well culture plate. CT images of the samples were acquired on a 256-slice multi-detector CT scanner (Brilliance iCT 256; Philips Healthcare, Cleveland, OH, USA). These samples were imaged with the following parameters: tube voltage, 100 kV; current intensity, 30 mA; slice thickness, 0.625 mm; scan time, 2.666 s.

The I-CQDs was prepared by an easy one-step pyrolysis of citric acid (CA) in the presence of iohexol according to the previously reported method with some modification [26]. The assumed process of formation of I-CQDs was shown in Scheme 1 . In this system, CA was used as carbon source, while iohexol as passivation agents. As shown in Scheme 1, with the reaction progresses from low to high temperature, the polymer formed first was carbonized and turned into CQDs. Subsequently, the surface of I-CQDs was functionalized with PEG diamine (PEG1500N) to afford the PEG-coated nanodots (I-CQDs-NH2) with terminated amine groups. The cetuximab was then covalently bonded to I-CQDs-NH2 using a modified EDC–NHS reaction [27], yielding the cetuximab-conjugated iodine doped carbon quantum dots (I-CQDs-C225).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure of the I-CQDs and I-CQDs-C225
ce:section.ce:para: Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images show that I-CQDs had a uniform size of about 3.7 nm (Fig. 1 A, B). The high-resolution (HR) TEM images show that I-CQDs have a crystalline structure of graphitic sp2 carbon atoms, and the lattice spacing is 2.3 (insert in Fig. 1A), indicating a grapheme lattice structure [28]. The sizes of I-CQDs were also measured by atomic force microscopy (AFM; Fig. 1C), and the average height is around 5.8 nm. Energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) of I-CQDs on the Cu plate without a gold sputtering process confirmed the presence of iodine element (Fig. 1D). The mean diameter and particle size distribution of I-CQDs-C225 were also determined by TEM image and DLS measurement. As shown in Fig. 1E, after modification with PEG diamine and conjugation with C225 on the surface of carbon quantum dots, the particle size turned bigger with average size around 6.9 nm (Fig. 1F). The mean size of I-CQDs-C225 measured from DLS is about 7.8 nm (Fig. 1G), which is understandably larger than that of I-CQDs, probably due to the conjugation of antibody on the surface of I-CQDs.

Fig. 2 A shows the XRD pattern of the as-synthesized I-CQDs. The broad XRD peak appears round 2-theta 22°, similar to the standard CQDs reported in previous work, which is consistent with the (002) lattice spacing of carbon-based materials, indicating the disordered stacking of CQDs with sp2-carbon structures.3 Fig. 2B shows the Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy of I-CQDs. A broad absorption band at about 3300 cm−1 is associated with the stretching vibration of NH and OH. The peaks at 1580 and 1675 cm−1 indicate the presence of CC stretching and carbonyl (CO) groups, respectively. These results confirm the existence of carboxylic acid and other oxygen-containing functional groups. The peak at about 1515 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration and bending vibration bands of NH, confirming the presence of amino-containing functional groups. Moreover, the absorption bands at 2930, 1410 and 1330 cm−1 can be assigned to the stretch of vibration CH, CC and CC, respectively, indicating the presence of alkyl and aryl groups.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was consequently carried out to determine the chemical composition of the as-prepared I-CQDs. As shown in Fig. 2C, the survey spectrum of I-CQDs have four peaks at 619.0, 532, 400, and 285 eV, corresponding to the characteristic I 3d, O 1s, N 1s and C 1s signals, respectively. The high resolution XPS spectrum of C1s can be deconvoluted into three peaks at 284.6, 286.1 and 287.7 eV (Fig. 2D), which can be ascribed to CC/CC, CN/CO and CO bonds, respectively [29]. This indicates the surface of I-CQDs was functionalized with hydroxyl, carbonyl and amide-carbonyl groups. Fig. 2E give the high-resolution XPS spectra of N 1s, which are deconvoluted into three peaks centered at 398.6, 399.3, and 400.3 eV, corresponding to pyridine derivative, amide N (CN), and doping N, respectively [30]. The O 1s spectrum exhibits two peaks at 531.0 and 532.3 eV for CO and CO, respectively. The high-resolution spectrum of I 3d (Fig. 2F) reveals the presence of 3d5/2 and 3d3/2 doublet at 619.6 and 631.1 eV, respectively [31]. This suggests that iodine exists in the form of negatively charged iodine in I-CQDs-C225. The iodine content of I-CQDs was evaluated to 1.13 wt. % by ICP-MS.

The optical properties of carbon quantum dots are very important for the applications of I-CQDs. The photophysical properties of I-CQDs were investigated. Fig. 3 A shows the UV–vis absorption, photoluminescence excitation (PLE) and emission spectra of I-CQDs. The UV−vis absorption spectrum of I-CQDs shows the strong absorption below 300 nm with maximum peak at approximately 243 nm, which is mainly attributed to the π−π* transitions of aromatic sp2 domains. Moreover, the weak absorption tail between 300 and 400 nm was also observed, corresponding n−π* transitions of heteroatom (N and O) containing bonds [32]. The maximum excitation wavelength of I-CQDs is 376 nm. The color of the well-dispersed I-CQDs is colorless in daylight, while the I-CQDs showed bright blue fluorescence under the irradiation with 365 nm ultraviolet (UV) light (inset Fig. 3A). The strong blue fluorescence can be observed with the naked eye, making them promising applications in a broad range of areas. In addition, the I-CQDs exhibits an excitation-dependent emission behavior within the excitation wavelength range of 300–430 nm (Fig. 3B), indicating that the emission of I-CQDs could be turned by changing the excitation wavelength. Using quinine sulfate as a reference, the quantum yield (QY) of I-CQDs in aqueous solution at an excitation wavelength of 330 nm was calculated to be about 18%, which is comparable to that of other reported carbon quantum dots [5]. After functionalization with antibody, the absorption and emission spectrum of I-CQDs-C225 did not show any significant change, as shown in Fig. 2C.

To get an understanding of the compatibility between cells and I-CQDs, a cytotoxicity study was carried out with a standard MTT (methylthiazolyldiphenyl-tetrazolium bromide) assay with HeLa cells. We chose the reaction time of 24 h to observe cell viability as a function of I-CQDs concentrations (0, 10, 50, 100, 200, 300, 400 and 500 μg/mL). As shown in Fig. 4 A, relatively high cell viability was still achieved after incubating HeLa cells with I-CQDs at concentrations of up to 500 μg/mL. Thus, the low cytotoxicity confirms the suitability of the fluorescent I-CQDs for bioimaging applications.

Considering the facile synthetic procedure and intriguingly strong PL of I-CQDs, investigating whether I-CQDs can be utilized as imaging probes for living cell imaging was worthwhile. Thus, the I-CQDs were introduced into HeLa cells through endocytosis and then imaged by laser scanning confocal microscopy. As shown in Fig. 4B, the HeLa cells treated with 0.2 mg/mL of I-CQDs exhibit blue (Fig. 4B(b)), green (Fig. 4B(c)), and red (Fig. 4B(d)) three different colors under 405 nm, 458 nm and 514 nm light excitation, respectively, which is consistent with the excitation-dependent PL behavior of I-CQDs. To further figure out the localization of I-CQDs in HeLa cells, Hoechst 33342 and LysoTracker® Green DND-26 staining were applied to visualize cell nuclei and lysosome, respectively. As shown in Fig. 4C, the red fluorescence from that of I-CQDs surrounded the blue fluorescence from Hoechst 33342, while was perfectly overlapped with that of green fluorescence of DND-26, indicating the iodine-doped carbon quantum dots are localized primarily in the lysosomes of cells.

We then studied the X-ray attenuation capacity of I-CQDs. As shown in Fig. 5 , the CT image brightness of I-CQDs increases simultaneously with the increasing I-CQDs concentrations. Compared with iodixanol (commercial contrast agents) reported in previous work [31], the CT image of I-CQDs is nearly equal to or brighter than those iodixanol under the same dosage of radiation at the iodine concentration of 0.16 mg/mL or above. The good X-ray attenuation capacity endows the I-CQDs with more sensitive CT imaging applications.

As reported in our previous work [25], HCC827 is a non-small lung cancer cell line which overexpresses EGFR, while non-small lung cancer cell line H23 and normal lung cell HLF show a low expression and a regular expression of EGFR, respectively. Thus, we used these three cell lines as in vitro model to validate the targeting properties of I-CQDs-C225. Firstly, the cytotoxicity of I-CQDs-C225 against these three cell lines was determined by MTT assays. As shown in Fig. 6 A, a very low cytotoxicity of I-CQDs-C225 was observed for HCC827, H23 and HLF cells at a concentration of 100 μg/mL. Even when the concentration of I-CQDs-C225 was elevated to 400 μg/mL, the cell viability of three cells is still about 80%.

To investigate the targeting properties of I-CQDs-C225, the cell imaging of three different kinds of cells was performed using laser scanning confocal microscopy. As shown in Fig. 6B(a–c), I-CQDs-C225 were efficiently internalized into HCC827 cells and shows a strong blue fluorescence after the incubation at 37 °C for 6 h. In contrast, a very weak fluorescence signal was observed in H23 (Fig. 6B(d–f)) and HLF cells (Fig. 6A(g–i)), indicating that I-CQDs-C225 could target specifically the cancer cells with over-expression of EGFR via EGFR mediated endocytosis. To further explore the subcellular localization of I-CQDs-C225, the fluorescence imaging was performed on HCC827 cells through a CLSM. As shown in Fig. 6C, after 6 h incubation with 100 μg/mL of I-CQDs-C225, the blue fluorescence from I-CQDs-C225 is perfectly overlapped with that of green fluorescence of DND-26, indicating that the cetuximab-conjugated iodine doped carbon dots are localized primarily in the lysosomes of cells.

In summary, a facile method has been developed to prepare bright luminescent iodine-doped CQDs by hydrothermal reactions using citric acid and iohexol as the precursors. Their aqueous solutions emit bright blue light under UV irradiation, and the PL wavelength was red-shifted under excitation with longer wavelengths. The presence of iodine ions in I-CQDs was confirmed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, which endowed the composite with good CT imaging performance. The I-CQDs can be used as efficient probes for cellular multicolor imaging and CT imaging due to their low toxicity, excellent biocompatibility, and good X-ray attenuation capacity.

Subsequently, an antibody cetuximab was conjugated on the surface of I-CQDs through PEG diamine to afford I-CQDs-C225. The bioimaging results showed that the I-CQDs-C225 could selectively enter the cancer cells (HCC827 cells) with over-expression of EGFR. Thus, the I-CQDs and I-CQDs-C225 we developed are capable of serving as a targeted fluorescence/CT bimodal imaging probe with combined advantages of high sensitivity and excellent spatial resolution. Ongoing experiments are being carried out to further improve this system for in vivo analysis.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are thankful for the support from National Natural Science Foundation of China (21601142) and Natural Science Foundation of Hubei Province (2017CFB689). X. Zhu thanks Hong Kong Research Grants Council (HKBU 22304115-ECS) and the financial support from Hong Kong Baptist University (RC-IRMS/16/17/02CHEM, RC-ICRS/15-16/02E and FRG2/14-15/034).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly photoluminescent carbon dots for multicolor patterning, sensors, and bioimaging
Near-infrared emissive lanthanide hybridized carbon quantum dots for bioimaging applications
Full-color light-emitting carbon dots with a surface-state-controlled luminescence mechanism
Green synthesis of bifunctional fluorescent carbon dots from garlic for cellular imaging and free radical scavenging
Facile synthesis of N-rich carbon quantum dots from porphyrins as efficient probes for bioimaging and biosensing in living cells
One-pot synthesis of gadolinium(iii) doped carbon dots for fluorescence/magnetic resonance bimodal imaging
